title: Whose Transition are we talking about - Conflict of My Thoughts
excerpt: I belong to a small coal mining district called Singrauli in Madhya Pradesh. It's small in geography but big in terms of its footprint on Earth — 140 MT of annual coal production, 21 GW of thermal power.
author: Abhinav Jain
content:
I belong to a small coal mining district called Singrauli in Madhya Pradesh in Central India. It's small in geography but big in terms of its footprint on Earth.

140 MT of Annual Coal Production. 21 GW of Thermal Power Production. This alone says a lot.

But what is the conflict in this?

1. I was living in the vicinity (less than 2 km) of a coal mine and thermal power plant and had seen 10-14 hours of power cuts during my childhood — and electricity in the Northern Coal Field Limited (NLC) colonies from my rooftop.

2. I saw every morning and evening, clouds of fumes coming out from the burning of coal by everyday workers and hotels for cooking, and claims of the government about the successful rollout of clean cooking solutions.

3. I saw my friends and relatives purchasing land and constructing houses which will soon come under new mines — and the ask of the international governments for coal phase-down/out.

4. I saw my known suffering from kidney disease due to increased heavy metal in groundwater — and claims of clean drinking water supply to every household.

5. I saw coal dust and ash getting collected on the home floor every 4 hours after cleaning, hazardous levels of air quality every day — and the claims of clean coal transportation, no road transport of coal and FGD in thermal power plants.

6. I saw the aspirations of everyone around me for a better quality of life, constructing new houses, purchasing new heavy earth movers machinery and trucks in the hope that they will be rented out in the upcoming mines — and the proposal of the world for Just Transition.

7. I was one of the many students who had to go out of town for higher studies — and the proposed models of successful Just Transition by converting old coal mining towns to educational hubs.

8. I saw no clue of even the smallest industry in the town — and the proposal of providing relevant skills for coal mine workers for an effective Just Transition.

I hope that my hometown will be a better place in future and all the best to everyone who is thinking and talking about coal mines, thermal power plants, clean air and Just Transition at COP this year.

Thanks a lot from all the people residing in and around the coal mines community.

Happy COP 28.

title: Solar Rooftop Calculator
excerpt: A practical tool developed by the Solar Rooftop Division at MNRE to help calculate the financial viability of rooftop solar installations.
author: Abhinav Jain
content: Solar Rooftop Calculator developed by the Solar Rooftop Division (MNRE). This financial tool helps homeowners, businesses, and policymakers estimate the cost, savings, and payback period of installing rooftop solar panels. Access the tool at: http://203.122.9.221/public/Grid/financial_tool/1
